title: Quantum Printing: Laguerre-Gaussian Beam Induced Topological Magnetic Textures
authors: Yuefei Liu, Tien-Tien Yeh, Alexander V. Balatsky
abstract: Structured light has become a practical tool for controlling matter by applying tailored, space- and time-dependent electromagnetic fields. We show that Laguerre-Gaussian pulses imprint non-collinear magnetic textures via the spatial structure of optical magnetic field. Our route offers a direct spatial selectivity determined by the optical features without relying on material anisotropic interactions. The proposed printing approach does not require interfacial anisotropy or bulk chirality, current-driven torques, or thermal quenching. We use micromagnetic simulations to demonstrate the potential to create topological charge density emerging during the pulse and reveal control through the optical topological properties and polarization. These results suggest structured-light quantum printing as a viable approach for magnonics and motivate studies toward reconfigurable topological textures enabled by ultrafast THz optics and non-thermal control.
summary: This paper proposes a 'quantum printing' technique where the spatial structure of Laguerre-Gaussian light pulses is used to programmatically create topological magnetic textures in ferromagnets. By tuning the spin and orbital angular momentum of the light, researchers can deterministically imprint complex patterns like skyrmions without needing specific material properties. This approach offers a versatile, ultrafast, and material-agnostic method for controlling magnetic topology.
main_problem: Developing a material-agnostic, non-contact, and ultrafast method to create and control complex, spatially programmable topological magnetic textures like skyrmions without relying on intrinsic material properties like Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Laguerre-Gaussian (LG) pulses can 'print' various magnetic textures (skyrmions, domain walls, multi-ring structures) by using optical quantum numbers (SAM, OAM, and radial order) to act as a symmetry-selected torque during an optical quench.
method: Micromagnetic simulations using the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation with a fourth-order Runge-Kutta integration scheme on a uniform Cartesian mesh.
model_or_system: A collinear ferromagnetic thin film modeled with a Heisenberg spin Hamiltonian featuring only intrinsic isotropic exchange coupling and an external time-dependent magnetic field induced by THz Laguerre-Gaussian pulses.
key_observables: Magnetization field (m_z, m_x, m_y), topological charge density, skyrmion number (Q), and spin angular momentum/orbital angular momentum transfer.
important_parameters: Optical quantum numbers (s, l, p), exchange coupling (J = 5 meV), Gilbert damping (alpha = 0.1), and THz pulse electric field strength (E0 = 1.5e10 V/m).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a simplified system with only isotropic exchange, deliberately omitting Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions, anisotropy, and dipolar fields to prove the fundamental printing principle.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of light-driven spin dynamics; Figure 2 illustrates the temporal evolution of the optical pulse and the resulting spin response; Figure 3 displays snapshots of magnetization (m_z) for different beam configurations; Figure 7 shows complex multi-ring spin textures and in-plane magnetization maps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of quantum printing, describes the physical mechanism of optical quenching and angular momentum transfer, presents micromagnetic results for various LG mode indices, and discusses the hierarchy of resulting magnetic textures and potential experimental implementations.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a unique way to use structured light (quantum optics) to control and program the topology of a many-body magnetic system, bridging the gap between optical angular momentum and emergent magnetic textures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) (a) Schematic of light-driven spin dy- namics in a micromagnetic thin film, where a structured optical beam transfers angular momentum to the spins. (b) Structured light carrying optical quantum numbers (s, ℓ, p, . . . ) imprints spatially varying non-collinear spin textures, generating topo- logical non-trivial (skyrmion-like), ring-like, domain-wall-like, and other complex magnetic defects.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Demonstration of optical pulse timing and spin response in this work, using a linearly polarized optical pulse as a representative example. (a) Temporal profile of the electric field Ex(t) used in the simulation. The representative times t1–t3 correspond to t/τEM = 10, 50 and 100, respectively. The shaded yellow region indicates the pulse-on interval. Right: snapshots of the out-of-plane magnetic field Bz of the optical pulse at the pre-pulse (t = t1), pulse-on (t = t2), and pulse-off (t = t3) stages. (b) Corresponding evolution of the spin texture at the same time frames: pre-pulse (t1), pulse-on (t2), and pulse-off (t3). The linearly polarized light induces...

FIG. 3. (Color online) Spin textures induced by structured light with different optical quantum numbers (s,ℓ,p). Snapshots near the pulse maximum at t2/τEM = 50 show characteristic magnetization patterns depending on the SAM (s), OAM (ℓ), and RO (p) of the incident light. The spot size of the structure light is 2w0 ∼400 µm, exchange coupling parameter Jj,k = 5 meV, and Gilbert damping parameter α is set to 0.1 for all simulations in this work.

FIG. 5. (Color online.) Imprinted skyrmion in an ex- change coupled ferromagnetic film. The optical mode is (s, ℓ, p) = (1, 0, 0) and the initial state m0 = (0, 0, −1). (a) Three dimensional rendering of the magnetization with color in- dicating the out of plane component mz, which is a snapshot af- ter the pulse at t3/τEM = 100. A reversed core with mz ≃+1 is embedded in a background polarized with mz ≃−1. (b) In plane magnetization map (mx, my) in the film plane, showing a circulating texture around the core. (c) Time evolution of the skyrmion number Nsk (black, left axis) together with the normalized drive envelope EG (orange dashed, right axis). The topological charge switches from Nsk...

Figure 5 (c) highlights a process of skyrmion printing. We see a clear separation of time scales between writing and relaxation. The transition to Nsk ≃1 occurs within a short temporal window set by the pulse-on envelope, after which the topological charge remains quantized even as the LG beam field decays to zero, persisting for more than 50 pulse durations. This persistence indicates that the system has entered a metastable basin in configuration space, so the subsequent dynamics is governed mainly by the topological protection and the slow energy dissipa- tion into extended spin wave modes in the surrounding uniformly magnetized region. As a result, the written texture behaves as a...

Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses the interface of quantum optics and spintronics by using the spatial and angular momentum properties of light pulses to drive and control magnetic textures.
